speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Joseph Weinreich. Mr. Weinreich, you were telling us about the colorful life in Chrzanow. Can you tell us a bit just briefly about your schooling there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. I was going to school. The school wasn't very far from us. I did not take a bus or anything like that, I just walked up the road. And I was going from 8 o'clock in the morning till 12:00, 12:30, to [INAUDIBLE] [? Shacknoff ?] [? Shanovia. ?] This is an ordinary school, which was run by the Catholic Church. There was nuns in there, and so forth. Opposite was actually a church. And we always-- the Jewish boys especially, always had the problems. A problem, because [LAUGHS] I remember as a child that-- that then-- when you saw the teacher, you have to take your hat off. You know, your cap. It was a special school cap. And we couldn't take of the cap because there was a-- the-- the church was next door and had the cross, and you're not-- you're not allowed to do it. But anyway, I went to school there. And I've got some very happy memories from school. And one [LAUGHS] memory, which of course I cannot forget, my day-- my first day in school. I was always fast. You know, I was always running. I was never walking. And I was running, and [INAUDIBLE]. You know, I-- I wasn't a child which was standing still in one-- in one place. And I went to school, and my class was on the first floor in the school. And I walked up-- the very first day, I think on the second lesson when there was a break, I was running in the corridor and somebody put his foot on me. And I fell over, and I broke my right leg in both places. And I was three months in [? gimps. ?] And I'll never forget the time because, you know, it was itching like mad. You know, on the-- on the bottom , it was itchy. And I couldn't scratch it. And my mother put a-- you know, on the window sill, she put some covers, you know, from the bed so it would be soft. I could sit on that and look downstairs and say hello to my friends [LAUGHS] when they were passing by. So the school was a happy school. I would say 90% were Jewish boys. So that was from 8 o'clock to 12:00, 12:30. Then I came home, and that's where my father was beautiful. He shut the shop every day and came home for lunch when I and my brother came home from school. And we were having every day a warm lunch together with my parents, my mother, my father every day, and this was beautiful. I loved it. The [INAUDIBLE] never had a time, but my father always had the time for us. And so-- and that was [INAUDIBLE] something like that. Then I went-- and this was contrary to any thing which a boy would do in a Hasidic home-- across the street, not far away, there was a [? polana. ?] You know, it was a big ground. And I used to play soccer on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was contrary to anything, you know, and I did it quietly. My father knew about it. My mother knew about it but they didn't talk about it, right? And I used to play soccer on there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I used to run home quickly, and have a bit of a wash, change, whatever, and went to the cheder. And the cheder was from 3 o'clock till 8:00. And again, my father was a beauty. I used to beg him, listen, father. Get me out of there, you know, about half past 7, you know, 7 o'clock. I've got to do homework for next day for school. Because I was-- I wasn't supposed to play soccer, I was surprised do the homework after lunch. And he used to come to get me out, so that-- that was was my-- my schooling. I had two types of schools, Polish, and Yiddish, and the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the majority of the people in Chrzanow were Jewish? There was also an element of non-Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there was Polish people there. And the Polish people there was-- were mostly-- mostly people which where-- how should I say-- laborers, and they were all employed in Jewish institutions. For instance, my grandfather, you know, he had a-- he had a-- you know, he was a very big merchant in wholesale, but he also had a retail for the city of Chrzanow. So they had a squat, you know, which was a warehouse, and there was a train going through the warehouse where they unloaded and loaded wheat. And he employed some Polish people to unload the wheat, because it was very heavy work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every Friday lunchtime, Grandmother, again, Grandmother, used to make a table. On the table was vodka, Slivovitz, and [? ikeloch, ?] so the-- the people which worked the whole week they ate and drank. Huh? So that was-- I see. And you mentioned also at school that 90% of the children were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was also non-Jewish people attending the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you get on with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. There was absolutely no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- so in Chrzanow, there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The school was closed, by the way, on-- on the Jewish holidays. This-- a Polish school was closed on the Jewish holidays, because nobody was attending. There was no one to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So life was very pleasant--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, as war broke out and became imminent, did you feel any change in-- in the atmosphere in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we didn't feel very much, because there was no-- there was no one-- the only change which I felt was the winds which came from outside the city, right? Like my-- my connection that anything was going to happen was through my uncle, which was in Palestine. Because he was begging us to leave the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because when he was married, my grandfather-- you know, he lived in Berlin. That's a whole story, but he lived in Berlin. And in 1933, when Hitler came to power, he went to Palestine with his wife, right? And I'm not sure whether the children were born in Palestine, or were born in Berlin. I'm not sure. And when he came in there-- so my grandfather was a rich man, as I told you-- so he sent them-- I can't remember from memory. My mother used to tell me 10,000 American dollars, and he built a house in the [INAUDIBLE] in Haifa, which stands still today, six flats. So Grandfather told me he build six flats. Anybody who needs to go in there can have a flat for their own, all the children, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is the wind which came from-- from Palestine, and he was begging him to leave the city. And Grandfather said, first of all, I can't leave all my [NON-ENGLISH], you know, my big, big properties and so on. And secondly, the [? Buvaru ?] doesn't allow me. Not that doesn't allow me, but he said that [? Buvaru-- ?] used to go to [? Buvaru ?] there was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. There will no-- will not be a war. And [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You'll stay there where you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandfather believed that that was to be the case?

speaker: interviewee
text: And believed that and that's what he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when war did break out, do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about your thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in cheder, right, or whatever I was-- anyway, the broke-- the war broke out. The whole city was screaming. There was a lot of commotion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody was running around, running around. The war broke out. What are going to do? What are we going to do? What was that-- what's that mean? You know, what are we going to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't want to call myself I was smart. I was more-- I was maybe, what shall I say. What would be the more appropriate English name would be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it doesn't come to me. Anyway, it'll come later on maybe. And I said-- I always had my-- a mind of my own. That was since childhood. I had a mind of my own. And I came to my father. I came to my mother. And I said, look, I spoke to some of my friends, and I see what's going on. And I know what's going on. Let's leave the city immediately, because we are from the border one hour from the German border. Once they breakthrough, they're coming here.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father say there won't be a war. You know, the Polish army will take care of them. It's only nothing. It's a small thing. And I said, no. No, I want to run. I want to go. Where you going to go? East, right? And-- so he looked at me, and I can never forget that. He said, do you really want to go? Let me find out who else in the family wants to go and you go with them. I and my-- my son, Moshe, will stay here to look after the-- after the houses, after the flats, after the business. He had a-- he had-- what's the name-- a whole business full of leather, you know, and so forth. He can't leave that, because people would steal it and then it will go to [NON-ENGLISH], it'll go to nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said it, so. So we made a survey in the family, of course, quickly. And one of my uncles, which is-- his name was [? Avrum ?] [? Hasch ?] Blumenfrucht. He said I'm right and he's leaving. He and his wife and two children, two of my cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is on your father's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's side. My-- my-- my mother's sister. Her husband-- you know, he was an extremely intelligent man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his surname was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blumenfrucht.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blumen is flowers, frucht this fruit. And Blumenfrucht is B-L-U-M-E-N-F-R-U-C-H-T. Blumenfrucht. And he was a very learned man, very intelligent man, so he said, OK. So he said, I'm running away together with my wife and the two children, and my two children. You can come, [? Freidal ?] and Joseph, you can come with us. And we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about moving away from your brother and-- and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you see, at home, you know, there was what we call a tremendous amount of [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, [NON-ENGLISH] means, you know, respect. And you don't tell your father, you know, anything that he's wrong or anything like that. Of course, I felt very badly about it, but I couldn't persuade him. I couldn't speak to him. Look, look, you're doing, the wrong thing. You know. I was only 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't feel well about it, but I accepted it, right? So we run away. And now, shall I continue with the journey? Tell us where you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So on a-- on a horse and a cart, actually two horses, and one of those Polish carts, a long cart, we loaded up a few things. My mother took a few things. I took a few things. My uncle and auntie [INAUDIBLE] and we were going to west Krakow. And Krakow was only three quarters of an hour away, one hour away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came in there and that's when we started-- that's when the bombardments started. That was horrendous. And you had to put the muzzle, and we were in-- I knew Krakow a little bit, because my brother used to take me to Krakow to the Jewish theaters there. So I saw the city but I was not-- I was not, you know, surprised. You know, because there's a-- there's a famous Polish song, you know, which it says I saw a cart without a horse, a tram. Because people used to come from the-- from the provinces, and when they saw a tram, they couldn't understand why it was moving. So I was not [INAUDIBLE] it. So we were there for a day, and the bombardment was colossal. Then at night, we started-- we moved up on the road. To-- we were going to Lwow. My uncle said we're going to Lwow. Now that journey, I will never ever forget in my life. The luftwaffe, you know, the Stukas, were bombing us from the back and from the front. So when they're bombing in the front, you couldn't get through, and when they were bombing away, you couldn't go back. Huh? And as a child, of course, I remember till today. The heads, and the limbs, and the-- and the torsos flying all over the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time there was a Stuka coming up, I could-- I was very, very quick. You know, I was very-- I always had my eyes open. I always was listening. I was always attentive. You know, I was-- I knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said straight into the road-- the road, you know, from the cart into the ditch, right? And-- but I saw-- I will remember always the horses when they were hit. The screams of the horses was something incredible. And the-- the thousands of people were killed. You know the blood was spilled all over the place. The limbs and so on. It was unbelievable. How did your family, your mother and-- and uncles, how were they coping with-- with what was going on? You were only a young man at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a young boy. Of course, you know, how they were coping with it? Well, it was tragic. It was a big tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, coming from such an affluent society, from such wonderful conditions, right, and we're finding ourselves being bombed. And we don't know every second of the day, you didn't know whether you are going to be killed or not. That you can end up like these people which were killed, right? So you didn't know. By a miracle, none of us got hit. None of us got hit. I always remember my little cousin, he used to get under the cart, right? But I said, no, you go together with me down the way. Somehow, I always took responsibility for things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And anyway, we-- we survived the bombardment, and we were going-- going on to that road, because there was only one road, one main road which was going to-- to Lwow. Later on, I was in Poland, and my wife and I-- I showed her the road, you know, where we were going actually in the same road. But anyway, that's a different story.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were going on this road, and after a few short days, the war finished in this respect. That the Russians came from the East, and they came to the River Bug And the Germans came from the other side, of course, from Germany, from this side. And Poland was divided in two zones. There was the German zone. There was the Russian zone. The Russian zone was smaller, of course, then the-- the-- then the German zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were caught in a place called [? Shemishel ?] on the side of the Germans. And there was a river going across, and we were caught in that when the war finished. And we looked around. We're alive. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the next thing we had to do is to find a place where to live. And I remember-- I cannot remember till today whether my uncle found it or I found it. I have got an idea that I found it

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to a Jewish-- to a-- to a home which was a wooden-- a wooden hut, a wooden place. And there was an old Jewish man-- in my eyes he was old. Maybe he was 50, maybe he was 60. I don't know-- with his wife, and I said to him, look, we just escape-- we just came from Chrzanow, you know, from-- and the war finished, and we've got to have somewhere to put our heads down, to lie down just for a few days. Allow us to come to your place. And he very graciously allowed us to come to his place. And remember his plac very well. It was one room. We were all in one room. My uncle, my auntie, my two cousins, my mother and I in one room, and it was even more primitive then Chrzanow. The woman had-- she used-- the lady of the house used to have a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a-- that was a casket, a wooden casket, like a 40-- 40-gallon casket. Like you've got-- wine, you know, like this sort of thing. She had water in it from the river somewhere she took the water. And carps were swimming inside. That was for-- for Shabbos, you know, for Shabbos to cook for Shabbos. And from that, we were eating. We were drinking that water. [LAUGHS] And was cooking from that water and we washed ourselves with that water. Everything from that little-- from that little thing. And we stay-- and the war-- and that's where we stayed when the war finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long we stayed there is approximately-- I would say two months. Approximately two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two months. And there where did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right, now that's a big story. That's a big story. You know, the Germans were very good. Straightaway, they have got [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, in Polish was a [NON-ENGLISH]. You mustn't do this, you should do this, you can't do that, if you don't do something like this, then you'll be shot, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So straight away, you can't go to a shop to buy whatever. So straight away, we're hungry. This was number one. There was no food. And then came another decree that was the most important decree. That was after one week we were there. Big placards everywhere. People which do not-- which were not born [? in Shemishel ?] and didn't live in-- before the war in the city have got to go back to the cities where they actually came from. Because there was a colossal influx of people into that city. All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they've got to come back. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So they got-- so they got to go back to [INAUDIBLE]. So of course, my uncle and my auntie and my mother, we all sat down, and we were contemplating to go back to Chrzanow. Well, there's a decree. You've got to go back. You've got to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And something horrendous happened and this turned my thinking immediately for not wanting to go back. And I'll tell you the story of what happened. As I told you, we were hungry. And I was very quick, right? And I had a friend next door living. You know, also same boy, you know, 12 years old, little boy like this. And we're thinking running to the market, running here running there, getting something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I saw a bakery, and there was an old boy, an old German fellow-- to my eyes, he was old. Maybe was 50, you know. And guards used to be-- they used to take elderly people to guard, you know, bakeries, or clothing, or whatever, you know, food-- guards. And he was sitting there in the sun on a-- on a box, and he was dozing. And I said to my friend, I tell you what we're going to do. I'm going to check him up, and you run-- and you get inside behind him into the bakery and steal a couple-- you know, some bread, so we have some bread your family and for my family, for our family. Fine, no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I spoke Yiddish, and the German spoke German, but we understood each other. Because I-- I German, right, and he understood Yiddish. Right? So-- [LAUGHS] because it's not such a big difference somehow in the languages. And there's a lot of Yiddish words from German, you know, origin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I was checking him up, checking him up. And in the mean time, I saw my friend getting in and getting some bread. He comes out with two loaves of bread in his-- you know, underneath-- he was wearing something underneath. And the German saw him, and he called him over. He said, you know, you're not allowed to steal bread. This bread is for the German-- for the German army. And I'm here for this reason. I'm here to actually supervise it, that you-- nobody is-- is stealing anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, he said, I can see that you're hungry. Fine, maybe I'll let you take it. But come, I show you something. And there was a box there, a wooden box. He said, you stand up on this box, he said. And he had a gun between his legs, right? And he said you look inside, right, and you'll see a bird flying out. Magic. All right? So my-- my friend got up onto the box and looked inside the-- the barrel. You know? He said, now what you've got to do is press this, and you'll see that immediately at once they will come out. And he pressed the trigger, and he blew his head off.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I run a home like crazy. That was the most vicious and the most horrendous thing that happened to me in [? Shemishel. ?] And I run home, and I was hysterics. I was in hysterics. And of course, I said to my-- then I said to my uncle, I'm not going back to Germany. I'm not going back to Chrzanow. What we're going to do, we're going to go across the river. We're going to go to the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of course, the river was not very wide, but it was a strong river. You know, it was-- the-- the-- the stream was very, very-- it was strong. And none of us could swim, of course, or very little. So then we were thinking, OK, how are we going to cross the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- as I told you, I was very quick, so I sat on the little hill. And I was watching that river. And I was watching, and there was a bridge, a wooden bridge across which was not bombed. It was still intact. And the Russians used to come from the other side to the Germans. They were [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to be friends, because at that time there was a pact between Stalin and Hitler, you know, and they were friends. And I saw the difference between those two people. The Germans prim and proper, beautifully dressed, lovely helmets, beautiful guns, machine guns, and everything else. And the Russians, jolly, they-- you know, dressed in-- in-- in [? sent ?] color, horrible looking shirts with belts, you know, and without a collar. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every moment they were dancing, and there were Cossack dancing. You know. And I said that's the life for me. That's good for me. You know, they're not going to kill us. You know? And we knew-- I knew already from school that they're Communists, you know, and that there's equality in Russia and that the Jews are equal with everybody else. [BACKGROUND NOISE] So I, said, no, we're going to cross the river, but to cross on the other side. But how do we go to the other side? I was watching it. Day and night, I was watching. One day, I saw the-- the most-- the most the feasible thing is either to go by a little boat, or to go on the bridge. But how do you do it? On the-- on the bridge, they stand centuries. They don't let you cross. And I saw one incident-- the-- the-- the Germans-- some Jewish people came down to the river bank, right? And they wanted to cross. And there was a little boat in there. So you want to cross? Eh, no problem. Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put them all in, much more than the-- the boat could take. They pushed them into the middle. He put a shot into the water and the whole thing sunk. So I said, well, this is out. The next thing what I saw was that from the German side, there was a column of Red Cross trucks. Three or four trucks with the Red Cross on it, crossing the river, crossing the bridge. And there were some people standing there in front, so the Germans started to scream [SPEAKING GERMAN] across, so that the column can go through. I said, uh-huh, there's an idea. And I was watching it, and watching it, and watching it. In the meantime, there was bigger decrees, bigger decrees. You know, you can't do that, and you can't do this. And they said, if we catch you in the street, that-- that you don't belong in here, we'll forcibly [BACKGROUND NOISE] you know-- and then it will be-- we'll kill you. Will do this and that the other one. And one day, I was sitting in there, and I saw a column from far away forming to cross the bridge. And I run home as quickly as possible, and I got my uncle and aunt. We had everything ready for that eventuality. You know. The little [INAUDIBLE], whatever we had. And the-- everybody was at home. Everybody was there, and we went in front of that column and the same story. [SPEAKING GERMAN], and we crossed the bridge.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you end up--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came on the other side. We came on the other side. Where do you go? So the Russians, you know, came-- a Russian officer, and a Russian soldier, and he said, what are you doing here? Right? You can't stay in here. It's the border, right? You've got to move on to Lwow, which is the nearest city. And we went-- part of the way, I think we would by foot and part of the way, we went by-- by a truck. We landed in Lwow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still together with your mother, with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother, my uncle, auntie, the three boys-- and the two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what date was this? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that must have been about two months after the war finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, so you're speaking now about mid-1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39. '39, the war started in-- in-- in the ninth month, in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been November. In '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1939, I'm sorry. And so you arrived in Lwow, and how long did you spend in Lwow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lwow, we were-- I think we were there for nine months. Nine months. And life in Lwow was not too bad. We-- we managed to get a room, the whole family together, one room. And we were all sleeping on the floor, right? There was a shortage of food. We were hungry, but it was bearable. Nobody was killing us and there was no decrees, nothing. You know, it was just plain sitting in there doing nothing. And I was running around the city. I knew every nook and cranny in Lwow from A to Z. I liked the place where our first president was shot. You know, there was a corner there where he was assassinated. There was a restaurant there, you know, next door. And-- and I had a cousin in there, which I met here in Australia later on. And so life was quite pleasant. And the thing which I remember about Lwow most is I had a bar mitzvah there. And-- and of course, I didn't have to get ready for the bar mitzvah, because from cheder you ought not to-- there wasn't a Bible. Whatever-- whatever parchment there was, I could read. It didn't make any difference. Not like you have to get ready for the bar mitzvah. A year ahead, [LAUGHS] I knew it, you know, straight away. I opened it up, and I could read it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which synagogue did you have your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little synagogue, and my uncle took me. And what I remember about my brother's bar mitzvah, of course, was that my mother got sick. And the doctor came, and she couldn't go with me to synagogue. And so he examined her, and he said Frau Weinreich, there's absolutely nothing wrong with you physically. The only thing which is wrong with you is you've got a broken heart, because my father wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went to the-- to the synagogue. I remember that little synagogue very well. I was standing up at the top, and I was reciting my little piece. And I went downstairs and everybody congratulated me. I went home-- there was nothing to eat, right? And then a cousin came to rescue. He was working at restaurant. He brought some food, right? And this is what I remember about Lwow. My bar mitzvah there. And when my son had a bar mitzvah here, I said I'm having my bar mitzvah together with you. Did-- were you able to maintain contact with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were completely cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So practicing religion was still OK for you at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was no problem. None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you maintain finance?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle took-- the only thing which he took with him was the money, right? So he kept us going. My mother had very little, and he kept us going. My mother had some, and he kept us going. But there was no food to buy, because you couldn't buy it. You know, you couldn't buy it. But look, it was bearable. I was hungry, but I-- it was bearable. The only thing which I suffered from is I terribly, terribly missed my father and my brother. That was the-- the hardest part of the lot in Lwow. Otherwise I was running around on the-- on the trams, and I was running into-- into the theaters and the cinemas, and so forth. And the Russians were jolly, you know, and-- and there was no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll take a break there.
